Browse by flow

Switch interview mode

3 flows
High-level design interview prep HLD
Architecture, distributed systems trade-offs, and end-to-end system walkthroughs for classic system design rounds.
Low-level design interview prep LLD
Object modeling, extensible APIs, machine-coding structure, and follow-up handling for class-design rounds.
Data structures and algorithms interview prep DSA
Curated coding-round practice across general problem sets and company-specific question banks for array, tree, graph, DP, and mock-round prep.

Curriculum → High-level design interview prep → Networking, edge, and request routing

HLD learning path

Networking, edge, and request routing

Learn how requests find your system, how traffic is distributed, and where you terminate policy, caching, and security controls.

Module progress 0%

0 of 5 lessons complete

Next up Lesson 1: DNS fundamentals

DNS maps names to endpoints and lets you steer traffic geographically, by health, or by weighted rollout without changing clients.

Why this module matters Build one clean mental model before scaling it.

Move through the lessons in order so each trade-off has context before the harder distributed systems decisions show up.

Study target 5 lessons left

Aim to finish one lesson, then explain its core trade-off out loud before moving on.

High-level design interview prep 5 lessons 0/5 complete Follow in order for best results Back to HLD roadmap Start module View lesson runway

Learning rhythm

Study this like a sequenced course, not a reference dump

  1. Preview the runway. Skim every lesson title first so you can see the progression before you dive in.
  2. Do one lesson deeply. Read the summary, inspect the diagram, and identify the sharpest trade-off.
  3. Say it back aloud. Explain the idea in interviewer language before you mark it complete.
  4. Only then advance. Move to the next lesson when you can defend the previous one without the notes open.
HLD sequence Designed for interview recall

Outcomes

What you should walk away with

  • Explain DNS, CDNs, and load balancers as separate layers
  • Choose between L4 and L7 routing based on needed controls
  • Use edge layers to reduce latency and shield origin services

Practice flow

How to study each lesson

  • Read the summary and explain the core trade-off in your own words.
  • Study the diagram and identify the critical path or failure boundary.
  • Answer the interview prompts aloud before marking the lesson complete.

Mastery check

How you know it is sticking

  • Clear assumptions and estimated scale.
  • Justified component choices instead of pattern dumping.
  • Awareness of operations, failure modes, and trade-offs.

Lesson runway

Follow the sequence from first principles to fluency

This is the core pattern borrowed from stronger learning products: show the path, make the next step obvious, and keep outcomes visible while the learner moves.

0/5 complete
Lesson 1 Next
DNS fundamentals

DNS maps names to endpoints and lets you steer traffic geographically, by health, or by weighted rollout without changing clients.

25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Lesson 2 Open
Content delivery networks

A CDN moves cacheable content closer to users and can absorb large amounts of read traffic before requests ever reach origin infrastructure.

25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Lesson 3 Open
Load balancing

Load balancers distribute requests across healthy backends and are the main control point for availability, failover, and safe scaling at the service edge.

25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Lesson 4 Open
Reverse proxies, gateways, and edge policy

Reverse proxies shape inbound traffic by terminating TLS, applying caching and compression, enforcing auth, and routing to internal services.

25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Lesson 5 Open
Rate limiting and edge protection

Protect critical services from abusive clients, noisy neighbors, and self-inflicted overload before the core system becomes unstable.

25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections

Lesson 1

DNS fundamentals

Open

DNS maps names to endpoints and lets you steer traffic geographically, by health, or by weighted rollout without changing clients.

Why it matters

Many high-level designs start at the wrong place. DNS is often the first layer that shapes latency, failover, and migration strategy.

What you will cover

What DNS actually doesTraffic steering patternsOperational caveats

Good interview signal

  • Explain recursive lookup, caching, and TTL.
  • Use DNS for coarse traffic steering, not every balancing decision.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 2

Content delivery networks

Open

A CDN moves cacheable content closer to users and can absorb large amounts of read traffic before requests ever reach origin infrastructure.

Why it matters

For global products, edge caching is often the fastest latency improvement and the cheapest way to reduce origin load.

What you will cover

What belongs at the edgePush vs. pull modelsTrade-offs and metrics

Good interview signal

  • Separate cacheable, immutable, and personalized responses.
  • Mention cache keys, TTLs, and invalidation.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 3

Load balancing

Open

Load balancers distribute requests across healthy backends and are the main control point for availability, failover, and safe scaling at the service edge.

Why it matters

They are foundational because almost every stateless tier depends on balancing to survive failures and scale horizontally.

What you will cover

L4 vs. L7 balancingHealthy routing and failoverOperational features

Good interview signal

  • Explain how requests are distributed and how unhealthy nodes are removed.
  • Choose L4 or L7 based on needed routing logic.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 4

Reverse proxies, gateways, and edge policy

Open

Reverse proxies shape inbound traffic by terminating TLS, applying caching and compression, enforcing auth, and routing to internal services.

Why it matters

They let you keep backend services simpler and more focused while centralizing cross-cutting concerns.

What you will cover

Reverse proxy roleAPI gateways and service exposureWhen to split concerns

Good interview signal

  • Differentiate reverse proxy, gateway, CDN, and load balancer.
  • Use the proxy tier for shared cross-cutting behavior.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 5

Rate limiting and edge protection

Open

Protect critical services from abusive clients, noisy neighbors, and self-inflicted overload before the core system becomes unstable.

Why it matters

Abuse resistance is part of system design; a product that collapses under spikes or bots is not production ready.

What you will cover

Choose the right limiting unitPlace enforcement carefullyPlan the user experience

Good interview signal

  • Name who is being limited and why.
  • Choose an algorithm that matches burst tolerance.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson
